# cat-task-manager

A TUI for managing small, repetitive daily tasks. Keyboard-operated, written in Rust.

*Note: Much of the following text was generated by AI and may be somewhat hard to read. It will be manually revised later.*

This application is not a general-purpose TODO app. Instead of handling projects, deadlines, priorities, tags, search, or complex dependencies, it prioritizes the straightforward progression of "today's fixed, sequential tasks."

## Philosophy

Daily tasks tend to become unsustainable if their management is overly elaborate.
What's needed is the immediate visibility of the task at hand, recording the start time, moving on upon completion, and lightweight operation.

Therefore, cat-task-manager does not include features that encourage adding too many tasks.
Tasks are not hierarchical. They have no parallel dependencies. They have no task-specific notes or attributes.
To maintain the daily flow, it strongly focuses solely on sequential progression.

Task definitions and the current day's interim status are exclusively maintained in `tasks/*.md`. Tasks are not written in the config.
It does not have a separate status directory.
Prioritizing the ETC (Easy To Change) principle for frequent task maintenance, it consolidates what the user interacts with into a single text file.

```text
- [ ] 朝のルーティン
- [ ] メール確認
- [ ] コードレビュー
```

In this format, when adding tasks, there's no need to replicate tables or field names.
The top-to-bottom order directly dictates the execution sequence, and changing the order simply involves moving lines.

## Background

As the saying goes, old habits die hard, and so does a task management app for daily life.

In the modern era of "vibe coding,"
there's no longer a need to conform one's habits to a task management app;
it's faster to "vibe code" a task management app that fits one's own habits.

That's what I thought, so I went ahead and "vibe coded" it.
It's not intended for others to use.
Destructive changes may occur frequently and without warning.
That said, I hope sharing this might provide some hint or inspiration to someone.

## Implementation Philosophy

Task definitions and their current state are consolidated in `tasks/*.md`.
Normal non-empty lines serve as task definitions, and their state is read as line-end JSON for each task line.
The line-end JSON is an area managed by the application.

```text
- [x] 朝のルーティン {"date":"2026-05-19","state":"done","started_at":"2026-05-19T09:00:00+09:00","completed_at":"2026-05-19T09:05:00+09:00"}
- [ ] メール確認 {"date":"2026-05-19","state":"in_progress","started_at":"2026-05-19T09:12:00+09:00"}
- [ ] コードレビュー {"date":"2026-05-19","state":"not_started"}
```

This is a deliberate choice to avoid duplicating state in separate files.
Even if a typo in a task name is corrected, the state is not lost as long as the line-end JSON for that line remains.
If lines are added, deleted, or reordered, the state moves along with its respective line.
Tasks marked `- [x]` without line-end JSON will be normalized by the app, adding line-end JSON with the detected time as both started and completed times.
If the line-end JSON format is malformed, it will be treated as an error upon startup or reload.

Statistical data from the past is read from the `tasks/*.md` files preserved in git history.
The persistent state of task information is stored solely in `tasks/*.md`.

The screen focuses on the next task, while also allowing a full overview when needed.
Upon startup, it displays a single line; `v` toggles between single-line, uncompleted, and full views.
The uncompleted view hides completed items, while the full view displays all items, including completed ones.
For completed items, the working time is shown, calculated from their start and completion times. Operations are only enabled for tasks that are not started, in progress, on hold, or deferred.

The number of states is kept minimal. It only includes: not started, in progress, done, on hold, deferred, and timed out.
"On hold" blocks the next task, while "deferred" allows progression to the next task without blocking.
When a task is on hold in the individual single-line tab view, a status explanation is displayed to facilitate switching to other tabs.
When the date changes, any incomplete tasks are treated as "timed out" in that day's record.
This is a deliberate simplification, not meant for complex deadlines or scheduling, but simply to record that "it wasn't finished today."

Operations are primarily keybinding-centric. Starting and completing use the same key, as do holding (to another tab) and resuming.
Deferring tasks is also toggled with a key operation.
`tasks/*.md` can be opened with a key operation, and reloading occurs upon closing.
It's faster to rely on one's familiar editor than to create forms within the app, and easier to inspect contents if something goes wrong.

## Configuration Structure

`config.toml` only holds editor candidates, keybindings, and startup git snapshot settings. It does not contain tasks.

```toml
editors = ["fresh", "zed", "nvim", "code"]

[startup_git]
auto_commit_and_push = false

[keybindings]
j = "next"
down = "next"
k = "previous"
up = "previous"
enter = "advance"
space = "advance"
p = "hold"
d = "defer"
q = "quit"
e = "edit"
l = "next_tab"
right = "next_tab"
h = "previous_tab"
left = "previous_tab"
v = "toggle_view"
s = "stats"
"?" = "help"
```

Only when `startup_git.auto_commit_and_push = true` will `config.toml` automatically commit and push `%LOCALAPPDATA%\cat-task-manager` once a day upon startup.

Save locations are consolidated under Windows `AppData Local`.

```text
%LOCALAPPDATA%\cat-task-manager\config.toml
%LOCALAPPDATA%\cat-task-manager\tasks\tasks.md
```

`tasks/*.md` is the Single Source of Truth that both the user and the app read and write. `config.toml` configures the operating environment.

## Deliberate Choices

This app is designed as "a place to progress through a fixed daily routine," not "a place to manage tasks."

For flexible TODO management, search, tagging, and deadline management, other tools should be used.
cat-task-manager is for starting each day the same way, progressing from top to bottom, and recording anything unfinished as that day's outcome.
Maintaining this simplicity is a top priority in its implementation.
